---
title: "Functions, Iteration & Efficiency"
execute: 
  eval: false
---

## Accessing the Lab & Data

Download the template Practice Activity Quarto file here: <a href="student/pa-2.qmd" download>`pa-2.qmd`</a> 

::: {.callout-important}
Be sure to save the file inside your Week 2 folder of your STAT 431 (or 541) folder! 
:::

## Writing Functions

1. Write a function called `summ_stats()` that takes a vector `x` as
input and produces the following output:

- for **numeric variables**: returns the mean, median, standard deviation, and
IQR as a data frame
- for **categorical variables**: returns the number of levels (categories) of
the variable

*Hint:* You can use `tibble()` to create the data frame. For example, 
`tibble(a = 1:2, b = 2:3)` creates a data frame with variables `a` and `b`.

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| label: sum-stats-function-soln

summ_stats <- function(x){
  if(is.numeric(x)) {
    tibble(
        mean = mean(x, na.rm = TRUE),
        median = median(x, na.rm = TRUE),
        sd = sd(x, na.rm = TRUE),
        iqr = IQR(x, na.rm = TRUE)
    )
  } 
  else if (is.factor(x) | is.character(x)){
  tibble(levels = n_distinct(x, na.rm = TRUE))  
  } 
  else {
    return(NA)
  }
}
```

## Iterating Functions

2. Use `map()` to apply your `summ_stats()` function to every column in the 
`diamonds` dataset. 
*Hint:* Look up the `bind_rows()` documentation from dplyr to combine summary
statistics for all quantitative variables into one data frame. The `.id`
argument will be especially helpful in adding the variable
names!

```{r}
#| label: sum-stats-map-soln
#| echo: false

diamonds %>% 
  map(.f = summ_stats) %>% 
  bind_rows(.id = "variable") 

```

## Data Transformation

3. Let's make the output more intuitive, with the variables on the columns and
the summary statistics on the rows. 

*Hint:* You will need to do a double pivot (`pivot_longer()` then
`pivot_wider()`) to achieve this result!

```{r}
#| label: double-pivot-soln
#| echo: false

diamonds %>% 
  map(.f = summ_stats) %>% 
  bind_rows(.id = "variable") |> 
  pivot_longer(cols = -variable, 
               names_to = "statistic", 
               values_to = "value") %>% 
  pivot_wider(names_from = variable, values_from = value)

```

## Using Helper Functions

4. Now that we have the output we want, let's use our code to write a 
`summ_df()` function that takes a data frame as an input and outputs a table of
summary statistics for every variable in the data frame. 

```{r}
#| label: df-function-soln
#| echo: false

summ_df <- function(df){
  df |> 
    map(.f = summ_stats) %>% 
    bind_rows(.id = "variable") |> 
    pivot_longer(cols = -variable, 
                names_to = "statistic", 
                values_to = "value") %>% 
    pivot_wider(names_from = variable, values_from = value)
}
```

5. Demonstrate that your function works using the `mpg` dataset. 

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| label: try-df-function

summ_df(mpg)
```
